steps.title: Wat Heb Je Gedaan?
steps.desc: Welcome to the past tense. Dutch uses the 'perfect tense' (voltooid tegenwoordige tijd) for almost all past actions in speech. It works like English 'I have done' — except Dutch uses it where English would say 'I did'. This is the most important grammar of A2.

steps.title: 🔑 The Perfect Tense: The Big Idea
steps.text:
To talk about the past in Dutch conversation, use:

SUBJECT + hebben/zijn + ... + PAST PARTICIPLE (at the end)

Ik heb gewerkt. = I have worked. / I worked.
Zij heeft gefietst. = She has cycled. / She cycled.
Wij hebben gegeten. = We have eaten. / We ate.

English splits 'I worked' (simple past) and 'I have worked' (perfect).
Dutch uses the perfect for BOTH in spoken language.

'Ik heb gisteren gewerkt.' = I worked yesterday.
NOT 'Ik werkte gisteren.' (This exists but is mostly for writing.)

The past participle goes to the VERY END of the sentence.
Same end-position as modal infinitives — you already know this pattern!

steps.note:
The grammar term. You don't need to memorize it.
Just know: past participle = the 'ge-...' word
that goes at the end of the sentence.

ge- + werk + -t = gewerkt.
The formula for regular verbs:
ge- + STAM + -t or -d.
Which ending (-t or -d)? That's the 't kofschip rule — next lesson!

steps.example: Zij heeft naar het werk gefietst.
steps.note:
ge- + fiets + -t = gefietst.
Stam = fiets (ends in -s, which is a 't kofschip letter).
So: -t ending. More on this rule in L2.

ge- + leer + -d = geleerd.
Stam = leer (ends in -r, NOT a 't kofschip letter).
So: -d ending. The rule becomes clear in L2.

ge- + maak + -t = gemaakt.
For separable verbs: ge- goes BETWEEN prefix and verb!
schoonmaken → schoon-ge-maakt.

steps.title: 📐 The Formula
steps.text:
Regular past participle = ge- + STAM + -t or -d

Step 1: Find the stam.
werken → werk
fietsen → fiets
leren → leer

Step 2: Add ge- at the front.
ge-werk, ge-fiets, ge-leer

Step 3: Add -t or -d at the end.
gewerkt, gefietst, geleerd

Which ending? Quick preview:
-T after: t, k, f, s, ch, p (the 't kofschip' consonants)
-D after everything else

Full rule in the next lesson. For now:
ge- + stam + -t/-d → past participle.
Put it at the END: Ik heb gewerkt.

steps.note:
Time word. Goes at start or after the verb.
'Gisteren heb ik...' = Yesterday I... (V2: verb stays second!).
Cognate of German 'gestern'.

You already know this from Unit 3!
van + daag = from + day.
'Vandaag' + perfect tense = talking about today so far.

steps.q: The Dutch perfect tense formula is:
Where does the past participle go?
For separable verbs, ge- goes:
In spoken Dutch, 'I worked yesterday' is:
steps.hint: Perfect tense for ALL spoken past

steps.title: 't Kofschip
steps.desc: The most famous mnemonic in Dutch grammar. When you make a past participle, the ending is -t or -d. Which one? Look at the LAST letter of the stam. If it's in 't kofschip (t, k, f, s, ch, p), use -t. Otherwise: -d. That's it. That's the whole rule.

steps.title: ⚓ 't Kofschip: The Rule
steps.text:
Past participle = ge- + stam + -t OR -d.

Which ending? Look at the LAST CONSONANT of the stam:

't Kofschip letters: T, K, F, S, CH, P → add -T
Everything else → add -D

Examples with -T:
werken → werk → K → gewerkt ✓
fietsen → fiets → S → gefietst ✓
koken → kook → K → gekookt ✓
maken → maak → K → gemaakt ✓

Examples with -D:
leren → leer → R → geleerd ✓
wonen → woon → N → gewoond ✓

⚠️ THE V/Z TRAP:
leven → stam = leef. F looks like a kofschip letter...
But the INFINITIVE is le-VEN. The original consonant is V.
V is NOT in 't kofschip → geleefd (with -d) ✓

reizen → stam = reis. S looks like a kofschip letter...
But the INFINITIVE is rei-ZEN. The original consonant is Z.
Z is NOT in 't kofschip → gereisd (with -d) ✓

Rule: when in doubt, check the infinitive, not just the stam.

deepDive.title: The V/Z trap explained
deepDive.text:
Dutch has a spelling rule called 'devoicing':
V becomes F at the end of a syllable.
Z becomes S at the end of a syllable.

So when you make the stam:
leven → leef (v devoices to f)
reizen → reis (z devoices to s)

The stam LOOKS like it ends in a kofschip letter.
But the underlying consonant (visible in the infinitive)
is V or Z — neither of which is in 't kofschip.

So: geleefd (not geleeft), gereisd (not gereist).

The trick: always check the INFINITIVE's consonant.
leven → V → -d → geleefd ✓
reizen → Z → -d → gereisd ✓

Or simpler: if the infinitive has V or Z before -en,
always use -d, regardless of how the stam looks.

steps.note:
koken → kook → K = 't kofschip → ge+kook+t = gekookt.
The double 'o' stays because closed syllable needs two.

stoppen → stop → P = 't kofschip → ge+stop+t = gestopt.
'Stoppen met' = to stop/quit doing something.

wonen → woon → N = NOT in 't kofschip → ge+woon+d = gewoond.
Final -d sounds like -t (devoicing), but you WRITE -d.

spelen → speel → L = NOT in 't kofschip → ge+speel+d = gespeeld.
Double 'e' in closed syllable = long vowel.

betalen → betaal → L = NOT in 't kofschip → betaal+d = betaald.
⚠️ No ge-! Verbs starting with be-, ver-, ge-, her-, ont-
skip the ge- prefix. 'Betaald', NOT 'gebetaald'.

steps.title: ⚠️ Verbs That Skip ge-
steps.text:
Some verbs DON'T get ge- in the past participle:

be-: betalen → betaald (NOT gebetaald)
ver-: verkopen → verkocht (NOT geverkocht)
ge-: gebruiken → gebruikt (NOT gegebruikt)
her-: herhalen → herhaald (NOT geherhaald)
ont-: ontmoeten → ontmoet (NOT geontmoet)

Rule: if the verb starts with an UNSTRESSED prefix
(be-, ver-, ge-, her-, ont-, er-), skip the ge-.

These prefixes are never stressed:
beGINnen, verKOpen, geBRUIken.

Contrast with SEPARABLE verbs (stressed prefix):
OPbellen → opgebeld (ge- goes in the middle)
SCHOONmaken → schoongemaakt

steps.note:
gebruiken → gebruik → K = 't kofschip → gebruik+t = gebruikt.
No ge-! The ge- prefix is already there (ge-bruiken).
Don't add another one.

verkopen → verkocht. Wait — irregular!
Not 'verkoopt'. The past participle is 'verkocht'.
Some verbs are irregular. We'll cover those in L4.

steps.q: Stam of 'werken' = 'werk'. Last letter K. K is in 't kofschip. So:
'Betaald' has no ge- because:
steps.hint: be-, ver-, ge-, her-, ont- skip ge-

steps.s: Ik heb rijst {1}. Wij hebben voetbal {2}.
steps.hint: I cooked rice. We played football.

steps.q: The stam of 'fietsen' is 'fiets'. S is in 't kofschip. So:
'Leven' → stam = 'leef'. F is in 't kofschip, so -t? No! Why?
opts: The original consonant is V (le-ven), V is NOT in 't kofschip
The rule doesn't apply to leven
steps.ans: The original consonant is V (le-ven), V is NOT in 't kofschip
steps.hint: The V/Z trap: check the infinitive

steps.s: Ik heb al {1}. Hij heeft {2} met roken.

steps.title: Ik Ben Gegaan
steps.desc: Not every verb uses 'hebben' in the perfect tense. Some use 'zijn'. Which ones? Verbs of MOVEMENT and CHANGE OF STATE. 'I have gone' = 'Ik ben gegaan.' 'I have become' = 'Ik ben geworden.' If you speak German or French, this system is identical.
goals: When to use zijn vs hebben
Past participles of komen, gaan, zijn, worden

steps.title: 🔑 Zijn vs Hebben: The Rule
steps.text:
Most verbs: hebben + past participle
Ik heb gewerkt. Zij heeft gegeten.

Some verbs: zijn + past participle
Ik ben gegaan. Zij is gekomen.

Which verbs use zijn? Two categories:

1. MOVEMENT (from A to B):
gaan (go), komen (come), vertrekken (depart),
aankomen (arrive), rijden (drive/ride),
fietsen (cycle), lopen (walk/run)

2. CHANGE OF STATE:
worden (become), sterven (die), groeien (grow),
blijven (stay/remain), beginnen (begin),
stoppen (stop — when intransitive)

⚠️ 'Zijn' itself uses zijn: Ik ben geweest. (I have been.)

German speakers: same system! Ich bin gegangen.
French speakers: same system! Je suis allé(e).

deepDive.title: The fietsen trap
deepDive.text:
'Fietsen' can use BOTH:

Ik heb gefietst. = I cycled. (general activity)
Ik ben naar het werk gefietst. = I cycled TO work. (movement A→B)

When there's a destination (naar, uit, in), use zijn.
When it's just the activity itself, use hebben.

Same applies to: rijden, lopen, vliegen, zwemmen.

Ik heb gezwommen. = I swam. (activity)
Ik ben naar de overkant gezwommen. = I swam to the other side. (A→B)

In practice, the Dutch are relaxed about this.
But 'gaan', 'komen', 'worden', 'zijn', 'blijven' → ALWAYS zijn.

steps.note:
gaan → gegaan. Irregular!
Not 'gegaand' or 'gegaat'. Just: gegaan.
Always with zijn: ik BEN gegaan.

komen → gekomen. Irregular!
Not 'gekomt' or 'gekomd'. Just: gekomen.
Always with zijn: zij IS gekomen.

zijn → geweest. Very irregular!
The most irregular past participle in Dutch.
Always with zijn: ik BEN geweest.

worden → geworden. Irregular!
Change of state verb → always zijn.
'Worden' is extremely common in Dutch.

blijven → gebleven. Irregular!
Stay = no movement, but it IS a state verb.
Always with zijn: wij ZIJN gebleven.

vertrekken → vertrokken. Irregular!
No ge- (ver- prefix). Movement verb → zijn.
De trein IS vertrokken.

steps.title: 📋 The Essential Zijn-Verbs
steps.text:
Always with ZIJN:
gaan → gegaan (gone)
komen → gekomen (come)
zijn → geweest (been)
worden → geworden (become)
blijven → gebleven (stayed)
vertrekken → vertrokken (departed)
aankomen → aangekomen (arrived)
beginnen → begonnen (begun)
sterven → gestorven (died)

Most of these are IRREGULAR.
That's annoying but predictable:
the most common verbs in any language are irregular.

Good news: there are only ~20 zijn-verbs total.
Everything else uses hebben.

steps.q: 'Ik ___ naar Amsterdam gegaan.'

steps.hint: She HAS come (zijn verb, zij = is)

steps.q: Which rule determines zijn vs hebben?

steps.s: Ik {1} thuis gebleven. De trein {2} vertrokken.

steps.q: 'Zijn' (to be) uses which auxiliary?
'Ik heb gefietst' vs 'Ik ben naar het werk gefietst'. The difference?
opts: hebben = activity, zijn = movement to destination
steps.ans: hebben = activity, zijn = movement to destination

steps.s: Wij {1} pannenkoeken gegeten. Zij {2} naar huis gegaan.

steps.title: Onregelmatige Deelwoorden
steps.desc: Some past participles don't follow 't kofschip. The vowel changes, the ending is -en instead of -t/-d, and you just have to memorize them. The good news: many match English or German patterns, and the 15 most common ones cover 80% of daily speech.

steps.title: ⚡ Irregular Past Participles
steps.text:
Regular: ge- + stam + -t/-d
gewerkt, geleerd, gewoond

Irregular: ge- + CHANGED STEM + -en
gegeten, gedronken, geschreven

The vowel in the stem changes (just like English!):
drink → gedronken (drink → drunk)
eet → gegeten (eat → eaten)
schrijf → geschreven (write → written)

If you know English or German irregular verbs,
you already know many Dutch ones.
The patterns are often the same.

steps.note:
eten → gegeten. Compare:
English: eat → eaten
German: essen → gegessen
Same -en ending, same pattern.

drinken → gedronken. Compare:
English: drink → drunk
German: trinken → getrunken
Vowel change: i → o. Same in all three languages!

schrijven → geschreven. Compare:
English: write → written (same pattern!)
German: schreiben → geschrieben
Vowel change: ij → e.

lezen → gelezen. The vowel doesn't change!
But the ending is -en (not -d).
Compare German: lesen → gelesen. Identical.

doen → gedaan. Irregular!
Compare English: do → done.
Same vowel change pattern: oe → a.

zien → gezien. Compare:
English: see → seen
German: sehen → gesehen
Short and common. Memorize this one.

nemen → genomen. Compare:
English: take → taken (different root!)
German: nehmen → genommen
Vowel change: e → o.

spreken → gesproken. Compare:
English: speak → spoken
German: sprechen → gesprochen
Vowel change: e → o. Identical pattern.

steps.title: 📋 The 15 Most Common Irregulars
steps.text:
HEBBEN verbs:
eten → gegeten (eaten)
drinken → gedronken (drunk)
schrijven → geschreven (written)
lezen → gelezen (read)
doen → gedaan (done)
zien → gezien (seen)
nemen → genomen (taken)
spreken → gesproken (spoken)
geven → gegeven (given)
vinden → gevonden (found)

ZIJN verbs:
gaan → gegaan (gone)
komen → gekomen (come)
zijn → geweest (been)
worden → geworden (become)
blijven → gebleven (stayed)

These 15 cover most daily conversation.
Memorize them and you're 80% there.

steps.q: 'Eten → gegeten' follows the pattern:
steps.hint: Irregular: -en ending with possible vowel change

steps.q: 'Doen → gedaan' — the vowel changes from:

steps.s: Ik heb een brief {1}. Wij hebben Nederlands {2}.

steps.q: Why are the most common verbs irregular?
opts: Common verbs resist regularization over centuries
steps.ans: Common verbs resist regularization over centuries
steps.hint: Same reason in English: be/was/been, go/went/gone

steps.s: Wat heb je {1}? Ik heb dat niet {2}.
steps.hint: What did you DO? I didn't SEE that.

steps.title: Gisteren, Vandaag, Morgen
steps.desc: You know how to form the perfect tense. Now let's anchor it in TIME. Yesterday, last week, this morning, two days ago — Dutch has rich time expressions, and they trigger V2 word order when fronted. This lesson also introduces 'morgen' for a sneak peek at the future.
goals: 12 time expressions for past, present, future
V2 word order with fronted time words

steps.note:
You know this from L1. When it starts the sentence:
Gisteren HEB ik gewerkt. (V2: verb second!)

steps.example: Eergisteren ben ik naar Utrecht gegaan.
steps.exampleEn: The day before yesterday I went to Utrecht.
steps.note:
eer (before) + gisteren = the day before yesterday.
Not common in English, but Dutch uses it frequently.

steps.example: Morgen ga ik naar de markt.
steps.exampleEn: Tomorrow I'm going to the market.
steps.note:
Also means 'morning'! Context tells you which.
'Morgen' (tomorrow) vs 'de morgen' / ''s morgens' (the morning).
Same word, two meanings.

steps.exampleEn: The day after tomorrow the weekend starts.
steps.note:
over + morgen = over-tomorrow.
Dutch loves these compound time words.
Very useful for planning.

steps.example: Vorige week heb ik pannenkoeken gemaakt.
steps.note:
vorige = previous/last. De-word adjective.
Vorige week, vorige maand, vorig jaar.
Note: 'vorig jaar' (het-word → no -e).

steps.example: Deze week heb ik veel geleerd.
steps.exampleEn: This week I've learned a lot.
steps.note:
deze = this (de-words). You know dit/dat.
deze = this (with de-words and plurals).
Deze week, deze maand.

steps.example: Twee jaar geleden ben ik naar Nederland gekomen.
steps.exampleEn: Two years ago I came to the Netherlands.
steps.note:
Goes AFTER the time expression:
twee dagen geleden = two days ago
drie weken geleden = three weeks ago
een uur geleden = an hour ago

steps.example: Hoe lang woon je in Nederland?
steps.exampleEn: How long have you lived in the Netherlands?
steps.note:
For duration questions.
'Hoe lang duurt de film?' = How long does the film last?
'Hoe lang' + time: 'Drie jaar.' = Three years.
Also: 'Hoe lang nog?' = How much longer?

steps.title: 📅 Time Expression Toolkit
steps.text:
PAST:
gisteren = yesterday
eergisteren = day before yesterday
vorige week = last week
twee dagen geleden = two days ago
vanochtend = this morning
vanmiddag = this afternoon
vanavond = this evening (already past)

PRESENT:
vandaag = today
nu = now
deze week = this week

FUTURE:
morgen = tomorrow
overmorgen = day after tomorrow
volgende week = next week

DURATION:
hoe lang? = how long?
drie jaar = three years
een uur = one hour

⚠️ When any of these START the sentence:
Remember the V2 rule from Unit 3? Verb stays second.
Gisteren HEB ik gewerkt.
Morgen GA ik naar de markt.
Vorige week HEBBEN wij pannenkoeken gemaakt.
Same inversion you practised with 'Soms werk ik...'

steps.note:
van + ochtend = from + morning.
Same pattern as vandaag (van + dag).
'Vanochtend', 'vanmiddag', 'vanavond' — all one word.

steps.example: Vanavond eet ik bij mijn ouders.
steps.exampleEn: Tonight I'm eating at my parents'.
steps.note:
van + avond = from + evening.
Can be past OR future depending on context.
'Vanavond heb ik gekookt.' = Tonight I cooked. (done)
'Vanavond kook ik.' = Tonight I'll cook. (planned)

steps.q: 'Gisteren heb ik gewerkt.' — why 'heb' before 'ik'?
opts: V2: gisteren is position 1, verb stays position 2
steps.ans: V2: gisteren is position 1, verb stays position 2
steps.hint: Fronted time word → verb second → subject third

steps.s: Twee dagen ___ ben ik naar Amsterdam gegaan.

steps.q: 'Morgen' can mean:

steps.s: {1} heb ik koffie gedronken. {2} ga ik naar de markt.
steps.hint: This morning I drank coffee. Tomorrow I'll go to the market.

steps.q: 'Twee weken geleden' means:

steps.hint: THIS WEEK I've learned a lot

steps.title: Hoe Was Je Weekend?
steps.desc: The most common Dutch small-talk question at work on Monday morning. This lesson is pure conversation practice: asking and answering about your weekend using the perfect tense. No new grammar — just fluency with everything from L1–L5 in real dialogue.
goals: Ask and answer about past activities
String multiple perfect tense sentences together

steps.title: 🇳🇱 Monday Morning in the Netherlands
steps.text:
Every Monday, every office, every school in the Netherlands:

'Hoe was je weekend?' = How was your weekend?
'Wat heb je gedaan?' = What did you do?

This is THE Dutch small-talk ritual.
You're expected to give a brief, pleasant answer.

Typical responses:
'Leuk! Ik heb gewandeld.' = Nice! I went walking.
'Rustig. Ik ben thuis gebleven.' = Quiet. I stayed home.
'Druk. Ik heb veel gedaan.' = Busy. I did a lot.

The Dutch value balance: not too exciting, not too boring.
Bragging is frowned upon. Complaining is acceptable.
'Het was gezellig.' = It was gezellig. (The perfect answer.)

steps.note:
Het-word. Borrowed from English.
'In het weekend' = on the weekend.
'Dit weekend' = this weekend.

The most common Dutch positive adjective.
'Leuk' covers: fun, nice, cool, pleasant, enjoyable.
You'll hear it 50 times a day.

Applies to people AND situations.
'Ik heb het druk.' = I'm busy. (literally: I have it busy)
'Het was druk in de stad.' = It was busy in the city.

Opposite of 'druk'.
'Een rustig weekend' = a quiet weekend.
Also: 'rustig aan' = take it easy, calm down.

THE untranslatable Dutch word.
Cosy + warm + fun + social + pleasant = gezellig.
A borrel can be gezellig. Oma's house is gezellig.
A rainy evening with friends is gezellig.
There is NO English equivalent. Just use it.

steps.example: Ik heb in het park gewandeld.
steps.note:
wandelen → gewandeld.
'Wandelen' = to walk (for pleasure/exercise).
'Lopen' = to walk (general/to go somewhere).
The Dutch walk A LOT on weekends.

steps.title: 💬 Weekend Dialogue Template
steps.text:
Q: Hoe was je weekend?
A: Leuk! / Druk! / Rustig. / Gezellig!

Q: Wat heb je gedaan?
A: Ik heb [activity] + past participle.

Examples:
Ik heb gewandeld. = I went walking.
Ik heb gekookt. = I cooked.
Ik ben naar de markt gegaan. = I went to the market.
Ik heb een boek gelezen. = I read a book.
Ik ben thuis gebleven. = I stayed home.
Wij hebben bij vrienden gegeten. = We ate at friends'.

Chaining them:
Ik heb eerst gewandeld, en daarna heb ik gekookt.
= I walked first, and then I cooked.

'Eerst' = first. 'Daarna' = after that. 'En toen' = and then.

steps.note:
Ordering events: eerst = first.
Goes after 'hebben/zijn' or at the start:
Eerst heb ik koffie gedronken.

steps.example: Daarna ben ik naar de markt gegaan.
steps.exampleEn: After that I went to the market.
steps.note:
Daar + na = there + after.
Perfect for chaining events:
Eerst X, daarna Y, en toen Z.

steps.example: En toen zijn we naar huis gegaan.
steps.note:
'Toen' on its own = then/when.
Very common in narratives.
'En toen?' = And then? (asking for more story)

steps.q: 'Het was gezellig' is the perfect weekend answer because:
opts: It covers cosy + fun + socially warm
steps.ans: It covers cosy + fun + socially warm

steps.q: 'Ik heb het druk' means:

steps.s: {1} heb ik koffie gedronken. {2} ben ik naar de markt gegaan.
steps.hint: First I drank coffee. After that I went to the market.

steps.q: 'Wandelen' vs 'lopen' — what's the difference?
opts: wandelen = walking for pleasure, lopen = general walking
steps.ans: wandelen = walking for pleasure, lopen = general walking

steps.s: Het was heel ___! Wij hebben bij vrienden gegeten.
steps.hint: Eating at friends' house = socially warm

steps.title: Een Typische Nederlandse Dag
steps.desc: A day in the Netherlands runs on a strict internal clock: breakfast at 7, lunch (bread) at 12, coffee at 3, dinner at 6. Sharp. This lesson teaches daily routine vocabulary using the perfect tense to narrate your day. Plus: the cultural phenomenon of 'avondeten om zes uur'.
goals: Narrate a full day in the past

steps.exampleEn: In the morning I drink coffee.
steps.note:
The 's = des (old genitive article, from 'des morgens').
'smorgens, 'smiddags, 'savonds — all one pattern.
The apostrophe is required.

steps.exampleEn: In the afternoon I eat a sandwich.
steps.note:
's + middags = in the afternoon.
Remember Unit 4: Dutch lunch = bread. Every day.
'smiddags om twaalf uur' = in the afternoon at twelve.

steps.exampleEn: In the evening we eat a warm meal.
steps.note:
's + avonds = in the evening.
This is when the real meal happens.
'Het avondeten' = dinner (literally: evening-eating).

steps.title: 🇳🇱 The Dutch Daily Clock
steps.text:
07:00 — Ontbijt (breakfast): bread, kaas, hagelslag
08:30 — Work/school begins (on time!)
10:00 — Koffiepauze (coffee break, sacred)
12:00 — Lunch: boterhammen at your desk
15:00 — Thee/koffie (afternoon break)
17:00 — Work ends (part-time culture: often 16:00!)
18:00 — Avondeten: the warm meal. Sharp at six.
20:00 — TV, reading, or visiting
22:30 — Bed (the Dutch sleep early by EU standards)

The Dutch are serious about 18:00 dinner.
'We eten om zes uur' is non-negotiable.
Being late for avondeten is like being late for a meeting.

The Netherlands has the highest rate of part-time work
in Europe. Many parents work 4 days, not 5.
'Vrije dag' (day off) is a cherished concept.

deepDive.title: Part-time paradise
deepDive.text:
The Netherlands is called a 'part-time paradise'.
60% of Dutch women work part-time.
25% of Dutch men work part-time.

This isn't seen as 'less ambitious' — it's a choice.
'Kwaliteit van leven' (quality of life) matters more
than career climbing.

Wednesday is often 'papadag' or 'mamadag' —
the day a parent stays home with the kids.

The result: Dutch children are consistently rated
among the happiest in the world.

steps.exampleEn: In the morning I eat breakfast.
steps.note:
Het-word. ont- + bijt = un- + bite.
Literally: 'un-bite' (breaking the fast — like English 'breakfast'!).
Dutch breakfast = bread, cheese, hagelslag.

steps.example: De lunch is altijd koud in Nederland.
steps.exampleEn: Lunch is always cold in the Netherlands.
steps.note:
De-word. Borrowed from English.
'Lunchen' = to have lunch (verb).
Dutch lunch = cold. Always. We covered this in Unit 4.

steps.example: Het avondeten is om zes uur.
steps.note:
Het-word. avond + eten = evening + eating.
The warm meal. The main meal. At 18:00.
Also called: 'het eten' (the food/meal).

steps.example: Om tien uur is de koffiepauze.
steps.exampleEn: At ten o'clock is the coffee break.
steps.note:
De-word. International word.
koffiepauze = coffee break (10:00, sacred)
lunchpauze = lunch break (12:00-12:30, quick)

steps.title: 💬 Narrating Your Day (Past Tense)
steps.text:
Chain events with time words + perfect tense:

's Morgens heb ik ontbijt gegeten.
= In the morning I ate breakfast.

Om negen uur ben ik naar het werk gegaan.
= At nine I went to work.

's Middags heb ik een boterham gegeten.
= In the afternoon I ate a sandwich.

Om vijf uur ben ik naar huis gegaan.
= At five I went home.

's Avonds heb ik gekookt en een boek gelezen.
= In the evening I cooked and read a book.

Notice: V2 rule on every sentence.
Time word → verb → subject → ... → past participle (end).

steps.q: The Dutch eat their warm meal at:
steps.hint: Het avondeten is om zes uur

steps.q: 'Ontbijt' literally means:

steps.s: 's Morgens heb ik ontbijt {1}. 's Avonds heb ik {2}.
Om vijf uur ___ ik naar huis gegaan.

steps.q: The Netherlands has Europe's highest rate of:

steps.hint: In the EVENING we eat a warm meal

steps.title: Unit 7: Alles Samen
steps.desc: Perfect tense with hebben and zijn. Regular participles ('t kofschip). Irregular participles. Time expressions. Weekend conversations. Daily routines. Everything from L1–L7 mixed together.

steps.title: 📋 What You Know Now
steps.text:
After Unit 7 you can:

• Form regular past participles (ge- + stam + -t/-d)
• Apply 't kofschip to choose -t or -d
• Know 15 irregular past participles
• Choose hebben or zijn correctly
• Use time expressions (gisteren, vorige week, geleden)
• Narrate your day, weekend, and week
• Have Monday morning small talk
• Understand Dutch daily rhythm culture

You're now A2.1 — you can talk about the PAST.
That's a huge milestone.

steps.q: 'Werken → gewerkt.' The ending is -t because:
opts: Stam 'werk' ends in K, which is in 't kofschip
steps.ans: Stam 'werk' ends in K, which is in 't kofschip

steps.q: 'Drinken → gedronken' is irregular because:

steps.s: Ik {1} koffie gedronken. Zij {2} naar huis gegaan.
Twee weken ___ ben ik naar België gegaan.

steps.q: Verbs with be-, ver-, ge-, her-, ont- prefix:
'Hoe was je weekend?' — the correct answer is NOT:

steps.s: {1} heb ik ontbijt gegeten. {2} ben ik naar het werk gegaan.

steps.q: 'Het avondeten is om zes uur.' Dutch dinner is at:
Separable verbs in the perfect tense: ge- goes:

steps.s: Het was {1}. Ik heb bij vrienden {2}.
steps.hint: It was gezellig. I ate at friends'.
